Scaling traditional businesses requires replication and duplication of the highest impactful things that somebody can do to create value for the business. Too many business owners live and die on being key person dependent in their organization. On the surface, this strategy seems to make sense. You have a talented, driven person who's great in their role. Your clients or your vendors love them and their peers respect them. They are dependable and accountable. You can't imagine an organization without them. Therein lies your problem. Your ability to transfer detailed processes and precise organizational information to your entire team on how to replicate your organization's most impactful conversion points has a direct correlation to your ability to profitably grow the business. All aspects of your life, your business, your teams, your clients, your customers, all of it. And we make that happen by creating active engagement through the rule of three. Here's how it works. Rule number one, define each process in your business. And when I say each process, I literally mean every granular thing your business does. There are required actions for everything you do. Nothing should be left in anyone's head. Collaborate closely with key stakeholders across the business in order to identify your business's processes. Pay close attention to who is and who is not providing valuable information. This will tell you more than you realize about your team. Rule two, document each process. It's one thing to know that you have a set of processes. It's altogether another matter of having them all documented. Prioritize documenting those processes first, which have the highest impact in the business. Pay close attention to the precise elements that matter the most to achieving success. Then drill, practice, and rehearse with these other teammates. Create a video series so you can train it to others. And that way, the optimal results that are achieved will be in video format. Then you can roll it back out to everyone in your organization and train them on how to do the exact same thing. New employees can use these operating procedures and training guides and videos, hitting the ground running with the information that is vital to their and to your company's success. Store these documents, videos, and processes in an accessible location. Organize them by department or by process and by system. And most importantly, identify those captains that you worked with so they have a living human example who can help them understand why they need to do what they need to do, when they need to do it, and what the expected result is. Put those people then in charge of future documentation, training, because that gives them an opportunity to grow and scale personally, professionally, and financially. In fact, start paying those people an override off of everyone they train. Watch how fast those key employees get excited about training new employees. Rule number three, develop your people than hire more people. The rule of three states that once you perfect something and document a process for it, perfect that process three more times through others. When your team is able to replicate those results, then have each team member train three more people and use your personal efforts working with each of them to dial in this become a developer of people developing people. The rule of three forces you to create processes, track and analyze data, develop leadership skills and understand what drives the business at a granular level to maximize the macro level financial abilities of the business. This is where massive value is created. This prepares you as a leader and reinforces with your team the importance of process, discipline and accountability.
